description: Architectural patterns for building ability and skill systems — GAS path vs lightweight custom approach

entries.title: Pattern: Ability System (GAS Path)
entries.description: How to structure your project when using the Gameplay Ability System. GAS is powerful but has a specific way it wants you to organize things. Follow this structure to avoid fighting the framework.
entries.whatItIs: The recommended architecture when you decide to use GAS. This is about where to put things and how the pieces connect — not how GAS itself works internally.
whenToUse: RPGs, MOBAs, action games with many abilities, buffs/debuffs, and attribute-driven combat
Multiplayer games that need networked ability prediction out of the box
When you need stacking effects, cooldowns, costs, and tag-based ability interactions
whenNotToUse: Simple games with 1-3 abilities and no buff/debuff system — GAS is overkill
Tight deadlines with a team that has never used GAS — the learning curve is steep
Prototypes where iteration speed matters more than architecture
keyPoints: STEP 1 — Decide where the ASC lives. For singleplayer or simple multiplayer: put it on the Character. For games where pawns swap (respawn, vehicles): put it on PlayerState.
STEP 2 — Create your AttributeSets. Group attributes logically: UHealthAttributeSet (Health, MaxHealth, Armor), UCombatAttributeSet (AttackPower, CritChance). An ASC can have multiple sets.
STEP 3 — Implement IAbilitySystemInterface on the actor that owns the ASC AND on the pawn if the ASC is on PlayerState. This is how everything finds the ASC.

STEP 4 — Create a base UGameplayAbility subclass for your project. Add shared logic here: input binding helpers, common tag checks, commit patterns. All your abilities inherit from this, not from UGameplayAbility directly.
STEP 5 — Define your GameplayTag hierarchy early. Plan categories: Ability.Skill.*, Ability.Passive.*, State.Debuff.*, Cooldown.*, Input.* — tags drive everything in GAS.
STEP 6 — Grant abilities in PossessedBy (server) or OnRep_PlayerState (client). Use a TArray<TSubclassOf<UGameplayAbility>> on the character that designers fill in the editor.
STEP 7 — Bind abilities to Enhanced Input through AbilitySystemComponent input binding or through a custom input component that calls TryActivateAbility.
STEP 8 — For each ability: create the ability class (activation logic), a cost GE (what it costs), a cooldown GE (how long before reuse), and any effect GEs (what it does to the target).
STEP 9 — Handle attribute changes in PostGameplayEffectExecute on your AttributeSet. This is where you clamp health, trigger death, react to damage.
commonMistakes: Skipping IAbilitySystemInterface — everything breaks silently because nothing can find your ASC
Forgetting InitGlobalData() in GameInstance — some GAS features won't work without it
Not planning your tag hierarchy early — retrofitting tags later is painful and error-prone
Putting GAS-specific logic in the Character instead of in Abilities and Effects — the Character should be thin
Granting abilities before the ASC is initialized — call InitAbilityActorInfo first
Using GAS for things that don't need it (opening a door, picking up an item) — not everything is an ability
goodPractices: Your Character class should be thin — it owns the ASC and grants abilities, but the real logic lives in Ability and Effect classes
One base GameplayAbility class for your project — shared patterns like CommitAbility, tag checks, montage helpers
Design GameplayEffects in Blueprints — they're data-driven and designers can tune them without code
Use GameplayTags as the communication layer between abilities — avoid direct ability-to-ability references
Create a debug HUD early that shows active tags, effects, and attribute values — GAS is hard to debug blind
Keep AbilityTasks for async operations (wait for montage, wait for target data) — don't poll in Tick inside abilities

entries.title: Pattern: Lightweight Ability System (Without GAS)
entries.description: How to structure a simple custom ability system when GAS is overkill. Uses components, enums, and cooldown timers for a small set of abilities.
entries.whatItIs: A minimal ability architecture for games with a handful of abilities that don't need the full GAS framework. Trades flexibility for simplicity and fast iteration.
whenToUse: Games with 3-10 abilities that don't interact with each other in complex ways
Platformers, shooters, or action games where abilities are simple (dash, double jump, ground slam)
Prototypes where you need abilities working in days, not weeks
When the team doesn't know GAS and the project deadline is tight
whenNotToUse: Games with 20+ abilities, stacking buffs/debuffs, and complex tag-based interactions — use GAS
Multiplayer RPGs where ability prediction and replication are critical — GAS handles this
When you know you'll need GAS features eventually — better to start with GAS than migrate later
keyPoints: STEP 1 — Create a UAbilityComponent (inherits UActorComponent). It manages a TArray of ability instances and handles activation/cooldowns.
STEP 2 — Define abilities as UObject subclasses (not Actors). Each ability has: Activate(), Deactivate(), CanActivate(), CooldownDuration, and an AbilityTag or enum identifier.
STEP 3 — Cooldowns are simple FTimerHandles on the ability. When activated, start a timer. CanActivate checks if the timer is active.
STEP 4 — The AbilityComponent exposes TryActivateAbility(Tag/Enum). It finds the ability, checks CanActivate, and calls Activate. Input binds to this.
STEP 5 — For costs (mana, stamina): the ability checks a resource value on the owning actor before activating. Keep resource management separate from the ability system.
STEP 6 — For effects on targets: abilities call ApplyDamage or directly modify target components. No need for a GE-like system unless you're building one.
commonMistakes: Building a 'lightweight' system that slowly grows into a bad reimplementation of GAS — if you keep adding features, switch to GAS
Storing ability state on the character instead of on the ability object — each ability should own its own state
Not having a CanActivate check — leads to ability spamming and broken state
Hardcoding ability behavior in the component — the component manages, abilities decide what they do
goodPractices: Keep it simple — if you're adding tag systems, effect stacking, or prediction, you're rebuilding GAS
Each ability is self-contained: owns its cooldown, knows its cost, executes its effect
The component is just a manager: holds abilities, routes input, checks activation
Use Blueprint subclasses for specific abilities so designers can create new ones without code
